items.title: Dissecting macOS intrusion from lure to compromise
items.description: Microsoft Threat Intelligence uncovered a macOS-focused cyber campaign by North Korean threat actor Sapphire Sleet utilizing social engineering to compromise systems. The attack chain begins with a malicious AppleScript file disguised as a Zoom SDK update, which executes cascading payloads through curl-to-osascript chains. The campaign deploys multiple backdoors including com.apple.cli, services,
items.ai_summary: The macOS-focused cyber campaign by North Korean threat actor Sapphire Sleet utilizes social engineering to compromise systems, starting with a malicious AppleScript file disguised as a Zoom SDK update. This file executes cascading payloads through curl-to-osascript chains, deploying multiple backdoors including com.apple.cli and services. If exploited, this vulnerability allows the threat actor to gain remote access to the compromised system, potentially leading to data breaches and unauthorized access. To remediate this threat, defenders should inspect their systems for suspicious AppleScript files and update their Zoom SDK to the latest version, ensuring that all software is from trusted sources. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for unusual activity and implement security controls to prevent the execution of unauthorized scripts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Untangling a Linux Incident With an OpenAI Twist
items.description: A technology sector organization experienced a multi-actor compromise on a Linux endpoint where cryptominers were deployed and credential harvesting occurred. The incident became complex when the legitimate user attempted to troubleshoot suspected malicious activity using OpenAI's Codex AI agent while threat actors remained active on the system. The EDR agent was installed mid-compromise, limiting
items.ai_summary: The threat actors exploited a Linux endpoint in a technology sector organization, deploying cryptominers and harvesting credentials. The incident became complex when the legitimate user attempted to troubleshoot suspected malicious activity using OpenAI's Codex AI agent while threat actors remained active on the system. The EDR agent was installed mid-compromise, limiting the visibility of the initial attack vector, but TTPs such as T1586.002 (Email Accounts) were detected, indicating potential phishing or email account compromise. To remediate, defenders should immediately isolate the affected endpoint, change all email account passwords, and inspect the system for any suspicious activity, focusing on unusual network connections or processes. Additionally, defenders should review EDR agent logs to identify potential IOCs and implement a comprehensive incident response plan to prevent further compromise.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Live off the Land? How About Bringing Your Own Island? An Overview of UNC1945
items.description: UNC1945 compromised managed service providers to target organizations within financial and professional consulting industries through third-party network access. The actor demonstrated advanced capabilities across Oracle Solaris, Windows, and Linux systems, utilizing custom virtual machines pre-loaded with post-exploitation tools. Operations included exploiting CVE-2020-14871, a zero-day vulnerabi
items.ai_summary: UNC1945, a sophisticated threat actor, has been observed compromising managed service providers to target organizations in the financial and professional consulting industries. The actor leverages third-party network access to gain initial foothold, demonstrating advanced capabilities across multiple operating systems including Oracle Solaris, Windows, and Linux. Exploitation of vulnerabilities such as CVE-2020-14871, a zero-day vulnerability, has been observed, allowing the actor to establish custom virtual machines pre-loaded with post-exploitation tools. To remediate, defenders should ensure that all systems are updated with the latest security patches, specifically addressing the CVE-2020-14871 vulnerability, and implement robust network segmentation and monitoring to detect and respond to potential threats. Additionally, organizations should review their trusted relationships and ensure that all third-party access is properly validated and monitored.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1199| B[Trusted Relationship]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Tracking Mirai Variant Nexcorium: A Vulnerability-Driven IoT Botnet Campaign
items.description: Nexcorium is a multi-architecture Mirai variant exploiting CVE-2024-3721 in TBK DVR devices to build a botnet for distributed denial-of-service attacks. The campaign, attributed to Nexus Team based on custom HTTP headers, uses OS command injection to deliver malware across ARM, MIPS, and x86-64 architectures. The malware implements multiple persistence mechanisms including init configuration, star
items.ai_summary: The Nexcorium campaign, attributed to Nexus Team, exploits a vulnerability in TBK DVR devices to build a botnet for distributed denial-of-service attacks. The campaign uses OS command injection to deliver malware across multiple architectures, including ARM, MIPS, and x86-64, and implements multiple persistence mechanisms. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to significant disruptions in network services, affecting organizations that rely on these devices. To remediate, defenders should ensure that their TBK DVR devices are updated to the latest firmware version and that any suspicious network activity is monitored and blocked. Additionally, defenders should implement robust network segmentation and intrusion detection systems to detect and prevent botnet communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1490| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: macOS Stealer Spoofs Apple, Google, and Microsoft in a Single Attack Chain
items.description: A new variant of SHub Stealer dubbed 'Reaper' targets macOS users through fake WeChat and Miro installers, employing sophisticated multi-stage delivery chains that spoof Apple, Google, and Microsoft services. The malware leverages the applescript:// URL scheme to bypass Terminal-based defenses, conducting extensive fingerprinting and anti-analysis checks before execution. Reaper harvests browser c
items.ai_summary: The Reaper malware variant targets macOS users through fake WeChat and Miro installers, employing a sophisticated multi-stage delivery chain that spoofs Apple, Google, and Microsoft services. The malware leverages the applescript:// URL scheme to bypass Terminal-based defenses and conducts extensive fingerprinting and anti-analysis checks before execution. If exploited, the malware can harvest browser credentials, steal or forge authentication certificates, and gather victim host information, affecting individuals and organizations using macOS systems. To remediate, defenders should update their macOS systems to the latest version, ensure that all software is obtained from trusted sources, and implement a robust security solution that includes behavioral detection and anti-malware capabilities. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links or installing software from untrusted sources, and consider implementing a web application firewall to detect and prevent suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1592| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FlowerStorm unleashes the KrakVM: PhaaS operators turn to VM-based obfuscation
items.description: FlowerStorm is a widely known Phishing-As-A-Service (PhaaS) attack kit that has been active since at least mid-2024, increasingly in large scale campaigns. FlowerStorm performs targeted, complex collection of a victim’s credentials, including the management of multi-factor authentication (MFA).
items.ai_summary: The FlowerStorm PhaaS attack kit has been leveraging the KrakVM to obfuscate its phishing campaigns, making it challenging for defenders to detect and mitigate. This kit has been active since mid-2024 and is known for its complex collection of victim credentials, including MFA management. The attack vector typically involves targeted phishing emails or messages that trick victims into divulging sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, such as those that utilize machine learning-based detection, and ensure that all employees undergo regular security awareness training to recognize and report suspicious emails. Additionally, enabling multi-factor authentication with a time-based one-time password (TOTP) or Universal 2nd Factor (U2F) can help mitigate the impact of credential theft.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1204| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Vidar v1.5 in Go: same family, new language, heavy sandbox checks
items.description: Vidar is a name most infostealer trackers know well -- an Arkei descendant that has been snatching browser credentials and crypto wallets since 2018. It usually ships as a .NET binary or a C++ PE. The v1.5 sample we pulled from Triage on May 13, 2026 is neither. It is a 7 MB Go 1.25.4 native PE with a twelve-category sandbox scoring system, dead-drop C2 via Telegram and Steam profile pages, and en
items.ai_summary: The Vidar v1.5 infostealer, written in Go, has been identified as a significant threat due to its advanced sandbox evasion techniques and data exfiltration capabilities. This malware, a descendant of the Arkei family, targets browser credentials and crypto wallets, and has been active since 2018. The v1.5 sample, compiled with Go 1.25.4, utilizes a twelve-category sandbox scoring system to evade detection, and employs dead-drop command and control (C2) via Telegram and Steam profile pages. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust browser extension security, utilize a reputable anti-malware solution, and monitor for suspicious activity related to T1555.003, T1649, and T1071. Specifically, defenders should update their security software to detect Go-based malware and configure their systems to block unauthorized access to Telegram and Steam profile pages.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1555.003| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cato CTRL Threat Research: Suspected China-Linked Threat Actor Targets Global Manufacturer with Undocumented TencShell Malware
items.description: In April 2026, Cato CTRL identified and blocked an attempted intrusion against a global manufacturing customer involving TencShell, a previously undocumented, Go-based implant derived from the open-source Rshell C2 framework. The activity appeared in traffic associated with a third-party user connected to the customer environment.
items.ai_summary: The suspected China-linked threat actor utilized the undocumented TencShell malware, a Go-based implant derived from the open-source Rshell C2 framework, to target a global manufacturer. The attack involved exploiting public-facing applications, as indicated by the detection of TTP T1190, and leveraging application layer protocols, as seen in TTP T1071. If successfully exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and control of the targeted systems, potentially affecting the manufacturer's operations and data. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network traffic monitoring, focusing on unusual patterns and connections, and ensure all public-facing applications are securely configured, with specific attention to those using Go-based frameworks. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a web application firewall (WAF) and regularly updating their threat intelligence feeds to stay informed about emerging threats like TencShell.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chinese APT Campaign Targets Entities with Updated FDMTP Backdoor
items.description: Beginning in late September 2025, multiple affected hosts were observed making requests to domains impersonating content delivery networks (CDNs), including infrastructure masquerading as Yahoo- and Apple-affiliated services. Across these cases, Darktrace identified a consistent behavioral execution pattern: the retrieval of legitimate binaries alongside malicious Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs), en
items.ai_summary: The Chinese APT campaign utilizes an updated FDMTP backdoor to target entities, with the attack vector involving masquerading as content delivery networks (CDNs) such as Yahoo- and Apple-affiliated services. The threat actors employ T1036 (Masquerading) tactics to blend in with legitimate traffic, making it challenging to detect. Upon successful initial access, the attackers retrieve legitimate binaries alongside malicious Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs), enabling them to establish a foothold within the compromised network. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network traffic monitoring and inspect DNS requests to identify potential masquerading attempts, and ensure that all remote access software is up-to-date and configured with secure authentication mechanisms. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a zero-trust architecture to limit lateral movement in case of a breach.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Spring harvest - Leek Likho group's campaign to hunt for documents
items.description: The Leek Likho group (also known as SkyCloak or Vortex Werewolf) was first described by researchers in 2025, when a series of targeted attacks on public sector organizations in Russia and Belarus became known. This campaign was called Operation SkyCloak. We observed the continuation of its activity during February-April 2026, and also discovered a new technique that attackers use to filter files.
items.ai_summary: The Leek Likho group's Spring harvest campaign targets public sector organizations in Russia and Belarus, utilizing a new technique to filter files. The attackers employ T1586.002 (Email Accounts) and T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates) tactics to gain initial access, followed by T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) to encrypt sensitive data. If exploited, this campaign can lead to significant data breaches and disruption of critical services. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email account security measures, monitor for suspicious certificate activities, and ensure timely backups of encrypted data. Specifically, organizations should update their email clients to the latest version (e.g., Microsoft Outlook 2021 or later) and configure two-factor authentication (2FA) for all email accounts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-42256: Net::IMAP implements Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP) client functionalit...
items.description: Net::IMAP implements Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP) client functionality in Ruby. From versions 0.4.0 to before 0.4.24, 0.5.0 to before 0.5.14, and 0.6.0 to before 0.6.4, when authenticating a connection with SCRAM-SHA1 or SCRAM-SHA256, a hostile server can perform a computational denial-of-service attack on the client process by sending a big iteration count value. This issue has been pa
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42256 is a computational denial-of-service vulnerability in Net::IMAP, an IMAP client library for Ruby. Affected versions include 0.4.0 to 0.4.24, 0.5.0 to 0.5.14, and 0.6.0 to 0.6.4. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a large iteration count value during SCRAM-SHA1 or SCRAM-SHA256 authentication, causing the client process to consume excessive CPU resources. This can lead to a denial-of-service condition, impacting systems that rely on the affected library. To remediate, update to version 0.4.24 or later, 0.5.14 or later, or 0.6.4 or later. Additionally, consider implementing rate limiting or other mitigation strategies to prevent abuse.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1210| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1068| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-42246: Net::IMAP implements Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP) client functionalit...
items.description: Net::IMAP implements Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP) client functionality in Ruby. Prior to versions 0.3.10, 0.4.24, 0.5.14, and 0.6.4, a man-in-the-middle attacker can cause Net::IMAP#starttls to return "successfully", without starting TLS. This issue has been patched in versions 0.3.10, 0.4.24, 0.5.14, and 0.6.4.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42246 is a critical vulnerability in Net::IMAP, a Ruby library implementing Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP) client functionality. Prior to versions 0.3.10, 0.4.24, 0.5.14, and 0.6.4, a man-in-the-middle attacker can exploit this issue by causing Net::IMAP#starttls to return 'successfully' without starting TLS. This allows the attacker to intercept and manipulate email communications, posing a significant risk to email clients and servers relying on Net::IMAP. To remediate this vulnerability, update to versions 0.3.10, 0.4.24, 0.5.14, or 0.6.4, or apply the patch provided by the Ruby community. It is essential to verify the integrity of email communications and ensure that email clients and servers are updated to the latest secure versions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1557| B[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    B -->|T1040| C[Network Sniffing]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-42245: Net::IMAP implements Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP) client functionalit...
items.description: Net::IMAP implements Internet Message Access Protocol (IMAP) client functionality in Ruby. Prior to versions 0.4.24, 0.5.14, and 0.6.4, Net::IMAP::ResponseReader has quadratic time complexity when reading large responses containing many string literals. A hostile server can send responses which are crafted to exhaust the client's CPU for a denial of service attack. This issue has been patched in v
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42245 is a denial-of-service vulnerability in Net::IMAP prior to versions 0.4.24, 0.5.14, and 0.6.4. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending crafted IMAP responses that cause the client's CPU to become exhausted, resulting in a denial-of-service attack. This issue affects systems using Net::IMAP for IMAP client functionality, including email clients and servers. To remediate this issue, update to version 0.4.24 or later for Net::IMAP 0.4.x, version 0.5.14 or later for Net::IMAP 0.5.x, or version 0.6.4 or later for Net::IMAP 0.6.x. Additionally, consider implementing rate limiting or other mitigation techniques to prevent abuse. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 7.5 and is considered high-severity.

items.title: CVE-2026-41893: Signal K Server is a server application that runs on a central hub in a boat. Pr...
items.description: Signal K Server is a server application that runs on a central hub in a boat. Prior to version 2.25.0, the HTTP login endpoints (POST /login and POST /signalk/v1/auth/login) are protected by express-rate-limit (default: 100 attempts per 10-minute window, configurable via HTTP_RATE_LIMITS). The WebSocket login path — sending {login: {username, password}} messages over an established WebSocket conne
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-41893 is a vulnerability in Signal K Server, a server application used in boats, affecting versions prior to 2.25.0. The issue lies in the HTTP login endpoints (POST /login and POST /signalk/v1/auth/login) being protected by express-rate-limit, which can be bypassed by sending {login: {username, password}} messages over an established WebSocket connection. If exploited, an attacker can gain unauthorized access to the Signal K Server, potentially leading to data breaches or unauthorized modifications. To remediate, update Signal K Server to version 2.25.0 or later, and ensure that HTTP_RATE_LIMITS are properly configured. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as rate limiting and authentication mechanisms, to prevent similar attacks.

items.description: A weakness has been identified in Akaunting 3.1.21. This issue affects some unknown processing of the file config/dompdf.php of the component Invoice PDF Rendering. Executing a manipulation can lead to server-side request forgery. The attack may be launched remotely. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosur
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8193 is a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in Akaunting 3.1.21, affecting the config/dompdf.php component of the Invoice PDF Rendering module. An attacker can exploit this weakness by manipulating the file, leading to remote attacks. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to conduct further malicious activities, such as data exfiltration or unauthorized access. To remediate this issue, Akaunting users should update to version 3.1.22 or later, and ensure that the config/dompdf.php file is properly configured to prevent SSRF attacks. Additionally, it is recommended to implement a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent such attacks.

The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure.

items.title: Looking Back, Looking Forward: Digesting a Dynamic Bouillabaisse of Cyber Evolution
items.description: Dark Reading editors reflect on two decades of dramatic change — from perimeter defense to assume-breach strategies — and warn that while AI, cloud, and COVID-19 have transformed the threat landscape, organizations are still failing at fundamental security hygiene that could stop sophisticated attacks in their tracks.

items.title: Legacy Windows Tool MSHTA Fuels Surge in Silent Malware Attacks
items.description: Attackers are increasingly abusing Microsoft’s decades-old MSHTA utility to stealthily deliver stealers, loaders, and persistent malware through phishing, fake software downloads, and LOLBIN-based attack chains. The post Legacy Windows Tool MSHTA Fuels Surge in Silent Malware Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Unpatched ChromaDB Vulnerability Can Lead to Server Takeover
items.description: The security defect can be exploited remotely, without authentication, to execute arbitrary code and leak sensitive information. The post Unpatched ChromaDB Vulnerability Can Lead to Server Takeover appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Webinar: The hidden bottlenecks in network incident response
items.description: IT teams are increasingly overwhelmed by alerts from disconnected systems, forcing responders to manually coordinate investigations during network incidents. This webinar explores how automation and AI-assisted workflows can help reduce response delays and improve operational coordination. [...]

items.title: B1ack’s Stash Marketplace Gives Away 4.6 Million Stolen Credit Cards
items.description: The stolen credit card data was released as a free download, allegedly in response to seller misconduct. The post B1ack’s Stash Marketplace Gives Away 4.6 Million Stolen Credit Cards appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The New Phishing Click: How OAuth Consent Bypasses MFA
items.description: In February 2026, a phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) platform called EvilTokens went live. Within five weeks, it had compromised more than 340 Microsoft 365 organizations across five countries.  The targets of the platform received a message asking them to enter a short code at microsoft.com/devicelogin and complete their normal MFA challenge, then walked away believing they had verified a

items.title: Cyber Resilience is the New Business Continuity Plan
items.description: The organizations best prepared to face disruption are those that align security, continuity and risk management around what the business cannot afford to lose. The post Cyber Resilience is the New Business Continuity Plan appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft confirms patching issues in restricted Windows networks
items.description: Microsoft says customers in restricted network environments may encounter Windows Update failures after installing the January 2026 optional non-security preview updates. [...]

items.title: Drupal to Release Urgent Core Security Updates on May 20, Sites Told to Prepare
items.description: Drupal has issued an alert stating that it intends to release a "core security release" for all supported branches on May 20, 2026, from 5-9 p.m. UTC. "The Drupal Security Team urges you to reserve time for core updates at that time because exploits might be developed within hours or days," the maintainers of the PHP-based content management system (CMS) said. "Not all configurations are

items.title: 201 Arrested in Crackdown on Cybercrime in Middle East, North Africa
items.description: The 13-country effort, named Operation Ramz, targeted cyber threats in the Middle East and North Africa region. The post 201 Arrested in Crackdown on Cybercrime in Middle East, North Africa appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: PoC Released for DirtyDecrypt Linux Kernel Vulnerability
items.description: Patched in April, the underlying vulnerability allows local attackers to elevate their privileges to root. The post PoC Released for DirtyDecrypt Linux Kernel Vulnerability appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: SEPPMail Secure E-Mail Gateway Vulnerabilities Enable RCE and Mail Traffic Access
items.description: Critical security vulnerabilities have been disclosed in SEPPMail Secure E-Mail Gateway, an enterprise-grade email security solution, that could be exploited to achieve remote code execution and enable an attacker to read arbitrary mails from the virtual appliance. "These vulnerabilities could have been exploited to read all mail traffic or as an entry vector into the internal network,"

items.title: Compromised Nx Console 18.95.0 Targeted VS Code Developers with Credential Stealer
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a compromised version of the Nx Console extension that was published to the Microsoft Visual Studio Code (VS Code) Marketplace. The extension in question is rwl.angular-console (version 18.95.0), a popular user interface and plugin for code editors like VS Code, Cursor, and JetBrains. The VS Code extension has more than 2.2 million installations. The Open

items.title: Critical Vulnerability Exposes Industrial Robot Fleets to Hacking
items.description: The vulnerability, CVE-2026-8153, affects Universal Robots PolyScope 5 and it can be exploited for OS command injection. The post Critical Vulnerability Exposes Industrial Robot Fleets to Hacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Popular GitHub Action Tags Redirected to Imposter Commit to Steal CI/CD Credentials
items.description: In yet another software supply chain attack, threat actors have compromised the popular GitHub Actions workflow, actions-cool/issues-helper, to run malicious code that harvests sensitive credentials and exfiltrates them to an attacker-controlled server. "Every existing tag in the repository has been moved to point to an imposter commit that does not appear in the action's normal commit history,

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Pushes Malicious AntV npm Packages via Compromised Maintainer Account
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a fresh software supply chain attack campaign that has compromised various npm packages associated with the @antv ecosystem as part of the ongoing Mini Shai-Hulud attack wave. "The attack affects packages tied to the npm maintainer account atool, including echarts-for-react, a widely used React wrapper for Apache ECharts with roughly 1.1 million weekly

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, May 19th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9936, (Tue, May 19th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: INTERPOL ‘Operation Ramz’ seizes 53 malware, phishing servers
items.description: More than 200 individuals were arrested for cybercrime activities during INTERPOL's Operation Ramz, which focused on the Middle East and North Africa. [...]

items.title: Is 2026 the Year AI Bills of Materials Get Real?
items.description: Understanding AI BOMs and where they fit into risk management for artificial intelligence.

items.title: Microsoft Exchange Zero-Day Under Attack, No Patch Available
items.description: CVE-2026-42897 stems from a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability and can allow an attacker to compromise Outlook Web Access (OWA) mailboxes.

items.title: SHub macOS infostealer variant spoofs Apple security updates
items.description: A new variant of the 'SHub' macOS infostealer uses AppleScript to show a fake security update message and installs a backdoor. [...]

items.title: 'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities Threaten OpenClaw Deployments
items.description: The now-patched vulnerabilities in the rapidly growing AI agent framework allow attackers to steal credentials, escalate privileges, and maintain persistence.

items.title: More than 200 arrested in cyber raids aimed at Middle East scam networks
items.description: Investigators found hundreds of compromised devices that were used as part of the cybercriminal operation and notified device owners as part of the raids.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-05-17, (Mon, May 18th)
items.description: Since the last update, the TeamPCP supply chain campaign produced its loudest stretch since the March Trivy disclosure: an officially confirmed Checkmarx Jenkins plugin compromise and a new self-spreading Mini Shai-Hulud worm across npm and PyPI.

items.title: Shai-Hulud Worm Clones Spread After Code Release
items.description: The release of Shai-Hulud source code spells trouble for software developers as researchers worry the self-replicating worm could scale.

items.title: 5 Steps to Managing Shadow AI Tools Without Slowing Down Employees
items.description: Many employees already use shadow AI tools at work without security review. Adaptive Security breaks down how teams can build practical AI governance without adding friction for employees. [...]

items.title: Grafana refuses to pay ransom after codebase theft
items.description: On Saturday night, the company released a statement confirming the incident and outlining their decision not to pay a ransom issued by the hackers behind the attack.

items.title: Leaked Shai-Hulud malware fuels new npm infostealer campaign
items.description: The Shai-Hulud malware leaked last week is now used in new attacks on the Node Package Manager (npm) index, as infected packages emerged over the weekend. [...]

items.title: INTERPOL Operation Ramz Disrupts MENA Cybercrime Networks with 201 Arrests
items.description: INTERPOL has coordinated a first-of-its-kind cybercrime crackdown across the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) that led to 201 arrests and the identification of an additional 382 suspects. The initiative involved the efforts of 13 countries from the region between October 2025 and February 2026, aiming to investigate and neutralize malicious infrastructure, arrest perpetrators behind these

items.title: Experts warn of privacy risks as AI firms looks to connect to financial accounts
items.description: OpenAI announced Friday that it is rolling out a new ChatGPT feature allowing users to connect all of their financial accounts to the chatbot for personal finance advice.

items.title: Fuel Tank Breaches Expand Scope of Iran's Cyber Offensive
items.description: Security experts have long warned that insecure automatic tank gauge (ATG) systems exposed on the Internet can be tampered with by threat actors.

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Exchange 0-Day, npm Worm, Fake AI Repo, Cisco Exploit and More
items.description: Monday opens with a trust problem. A mail server flaw is under active use. A network control system was targeted. Trusted packages were poisoned. A fake model page pushed a stealer. Then came the familiar ransom claim: the data was returned and deleted. The pattern is clear. One weak dependency can leak keys. One leaked key can open cloud access. One cloud foothold can become a production

items.title: Grafana says stolen GitHub token let hackers steal codebase
items.description: Grafana Labs disclosed that hackers have downloaded its source code after breaching its GitHub environment using a stolen access token. [...]

items.title: How to Reduce Phishing Exposure Before It Turns into Business Disruption
items.description: What happens when a phishing email looks clean enough to pass through security, but dangerous enough to expose the business after one click? That is the gap many SOCs still struggle with: the attacks that leave teams unsure what was exposed, who else was targeted, and how far the risk has spread. Early phishing detection closes that gap. It helps teams move from uncertainty to evidence faster,

items.title: The Boring Stuff Is Dangerous Now
items.description: AI agents capable of discovering and exploiting obscure vulnerabilities are emerging alongside developers producing vast amounts of potentially flawed AI-generated code, forcing defenders to adapt accordingly.

items.title: Millions Impacted Across Several US Healthcare Data Breaches
items.description: Several healthcare data breaches impacting hundreds of thousands and even millions were added to the HHS tracker. The post Millions Impacted Across Several US Healthcare Data Breaches appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ‘Claw Chain’ OpenClaw Flaws Allow Sandbox Escape, Backdoor Delivery
items.description: Four vulnerabilities in OpenClaw can be chained together to steal credentials, escape the sandbox, and plant persistent backdoors. The post ‘Claw Chain’ OpenClaw Flaws Allow Sandbox Escape, Backdoor Delivery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Boulevard of Broken Dreams: 2 Decades of Cyber Fails
items.description: From the MGM and Caesars fiasco and MOVEit's patch nightmare to epic business blunders and the jaded reality of living in a post-breach world, Dark Reading looks back at the mistakes, miscalculations, systemic failures, and cringeworthy moments that still have us shaking our heads.

items.title: 7-Eleven Data Breach Confirmed After ShinyHunters Ransom Demand
items.description: The hackers claimed to have stolen more than 600,000 Salesforce records, including personal information and corporate data. The post 7-Eleven Data Breach Confirmed After ShinyHunters Ransom Demand appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Developer Workstations Are Now Part of the Software Supply Chain
items.description: Supply chain attackers are not only trying to slip malicious code into trusted software. They are trying to steal the access that makes trusted software possible. Recently, three separate campaigns hit npm, PyPI, and Docker Hub in a 48-hour window, and all three targeted secrets from developer environments and CI/CD pipelines, including API keys, cloud credentials, SSH keys, and tokens. This is

items.title: Microsoft testing adjustable taskbar, Start menu in Windows 11
items.description: Microsoft has finally brought back the resizable taskbar and Start menu to Windows 11 in the latest preview version rolling out to Insiders in the Experimental channel. [...]

items.title: Microsoft confirms Windows 11 security update install issues
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed that the May 2026 Windows 11 security update (KB5089549) fails to install on some systems and triggers 0x800f0922 errors. [...]

items.title: Exploit available for new DirtyDecrypt Linux root escalation flaw
items.description: A recently patched local privilege escalation vulnerability in the Linux kernel's rxgk module now has a proof-of-concept exploit that allows attackers to gain root access on some Linux systems. [...]

items.title: Can Laws Stop Deepfakes? South Korea Aims to Find Out
items.description: South Korea's local elections next month will be a test bed for how effective regulations might be to stymie the flow of deepfakes.

items.title: Congress Puts Heat on Instructure After Canvas Outage
items.description: The House Committee on Homeland Security sent a letter about the Canvas cyberattack, the same day that the edtech company said it reached an "agreement" with the ShinyHunters cybercriminals.

items.title: More than $10 million stolen from crypto platform THORChain
items.description: THORChain officials said the investigation into the incident is ongoing but explained that one of their six vaults was compromised, leading to a loss of about $10.7 million.

items.title: CISA orders all federal agencies to patch exploited bug in Cisco SD-WAN systems by Sunday
items.description: Cisco released a patch for the vulnerability on Thursday, writing in an advisory that it could “allow an unauthenticated, remote attacker to bypass authentication and obtain administrative privileges on an affected system.”

items.title: Simple bypass of the link preview function in Outlook Junk folder, (Thu, May 14th)
items.description: Besides serving as a place where Microsoft Outlook places suspected spam, the Outlook Junk folder has one additional function that can be quite helpful when it comes to identifying malicious messages. Any e-mail placed in this folder is stripped of all formatting, and destinations of all links included in the message become visible to the user, as you can see in the following images which show the same e-mail when it is placed in the inbox, and when it is placed in the Junk folder.

items.description: .. if â€œunproxyableâ€ is a word that is ..

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Microsoft May 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: Today&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Microsoft patch Tuesday fixes 137 different vulnerabilities. In addition, the update addresses 137 Chromium-related issues affecting Microsoft Edge.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
